So, we've got:
Start -

Step 1 - read hook letter, and receive bedroom riddle, proceed to bedroom. (note the bedroom riddle is given only in the starting area, and so the prose in step 2 - Bedroom, is no longer necessary, they get that in step 1)
Step 2 - arrive in bedroom, find map fragment F1 by the bed, with note telling them to look for another location clue here (âBut something else is left behindâ¦ A further clue for you to find.â). Find location clue. They ate in haste before they fled, Search where sailors might be fed. Go to dining room (or kitchen, depending on the house setup)
Step 3 - Pre branch. In dining room, find fragment F2. (note: do we want to directly find the fragment here, or find a clue that leads to the fragment - i prefer clue -> fragment personally) Find Branch choice. Make Choice.
Step 4 + 5 - Paths. Follow path in accordance with step 3 choice.
Step 6 - both paths lead you to the same conclusion/location (note: where?). Players find a map fragment F3, and their final clue.
Step 7 - Final clue, one short step for the last map fragment F4. (note: we need something for this, i don't think we have that yet)
Step 8 - Assemble the map pieces, add character cards, reference the winning location from the 4 Locations sheets (jungle, lagoon, volcano, rocks or whatever). Find winning location, receive treasure, win.
Note: we have not covered the references to the Location Sheets in the prose or instructions, or decided where those mentions will go. we need that next otherwise the final "aha!" moment is missed.

wait, it can't work like that because we need the final cypher fragment to reveal the lodcation of the final map fragment, which reveals which location sheet the treasure is hidden at.
Yes â exactly. Thatâs the structural limit.
Youâve caught the key dependency:

The cipher cannot sit after the map is complete
because the cipherâs job is to reveal where F4 is, and F4 is needed for the final map solve.
So the sequence has to be:

collect early fragments and cipher fragments
assemble the cipher
decode clue to find F4
assemble full map
place character cards
identify winning location sheet
find treasure
That means the âspecial seek tasksâ idea can still work, but the cipher fragments must be fully available before the F4 step.

Clean corrected structure
What the cipher should do
Not reveal the treasure location directly.
It should reveal:
the location of the final map fragment F4
Then:

F4 completes the map
the completed map + character cards reveal the winning landmark
that landmark maps to the location sheet
treasure is there
That is the correct hierarchy.

Best implementation
Distribution
Step 2: main group gets F1, one player gets Cipher Fragment A
Step 3: main group gets F2, one player gets Cipher Fragment B
Step 4/5 branch path: no map fragment, but one player gets Cipher Fragment C
Step 6 convergence: main group gets F3, one player gets Cipher Fragment D
Step 7: combined cipher now reveals where F4 is hidden
Step 8: find F4
Step 9: assemble map + place crew
Step 10: identify location sheet + treasure
That works.
Why this is better
It preserves all the good stuff:

rotating player spotlight
collaborative payoff
delayed-use item system
final map remains the real final puzzle
And it avoids the broken version where:

the cipher competes with the map reveal
or the final clue comes too late to matter
Important design rule
The cipher fragments should not be hard puzzles themselves.
They should be:

quick finds
special mini-objectives
easy to retrieve
The actual thinking happens when the group combines them into the final cipher that points to F4.
The clean mental model
Think of it as two parallel tracks:
Track A â Main hunt
F1 â F2 â F3 â F4
Track B â Hidden support system
Cipher A â Cipher B â Cipher C â Cipher D
At Step 7, Track B unlocks Track Aâs final missing piece.
Then Track A unlocks the treasure.
What this means for your current design
You do not need to redesign the whole hunt.
You just need to reposition the cipher system so that it supports the discovery of F4, not the treasure itself.

Updated high-level flow
Start: hook letter + bedroom clue
Bedroom: find F1 + special player finds Cipher A
Dining/pre-branch: find F2 + special player finds Cipher B
Branch path: solve path puzzle + special player finds Cipher C
Convergence: find F3 + special player finds Cipher D
Cipher assembly: decode clue to final hiding place
Find F4
Assemble map
Place character cards
Identify landmark
Go to matching location sheet
Find treasure
That is now logically correct.
The next thing to do is lock where Cipher A/B/C/D appear and what exact format the combined cipher takes.
